target.title: fix(gateway): surface approval delivery failures
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #89217.
- Problem: explicit exec/plugin approval delivery failures were flattened to `false`, so `handlePendingApprovalRequest()` could not distinguish "no configured delivery route" from "a configured delivery path was tried and failed". When the originating turn source could route approvals, that failure silently fell back to pending turn-source routing instead of surfacing `no-approval-route`.
- Solution: carry delivery result metadata as `{ delivered, attempted }` through shared approval handling, exec approval forwarding, plugin approval forwarding, and iOS push delivery. A configured delivery path that was attempted and failed now expires the approval with `resolvedBy: "no-approval-route"` and `decision: null`; an unconfigured delivery path still allows the existing turn-source fallback.
- What did NOT change: this does not add a new approval channel, change approval decisions, change approval request IDs or payload fields, remove turn-source routing, or alter the unconfigured-delivery fallback. It only changes the route/no-route classification for explicit delivery attempts that already ran and failed.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: the source-of-truth boundary is the shared gateway approval delivery contract consumed by `handlePendingApprovalRequest()`. Exec forwarding, plugin forwarding, and iOS push now report the same canonical `{ delivered, attempted }` state to that shared contract; channel-specific code does not decide the final pending/no-route lifecycle state.
- Related open PR scan: daily-fix issue-mode setup did not identify an open competing fix for #89217 before this branch was prepared.
- Out of scope: no channel-specific retry policy, APNs/Slack/Discord transport behavior, approval UI, approval decision semantics, or plugin schema/config migration is changed in this PR.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the gateway approval lifecycle invariant was violated because the delivery contract used one boolean for two different states: no configured route and configured route attempted but failed. That lossy contract caused the shared pending-approval state machine to treat explicit delivery failure as if no delivery had been attempted.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the source-of-truth delivery contract now carries the invariant explicitly as `{ delivered, attempted }`, so `handlePendingApprovalRequest()` can reject attempted explicit delivery failures while preserving the unchanged turn-source fallback for unattempted delivery. This fixes the state/lifecycle cause instead of adding a downstream special case for one channel.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression coverage for an explicit delivery failure when an originating turn-source route was also available.
- `handlePendingApprovalRequest()` treated `false` plus an available turn-source route as safe to keep pending, even after a real explicit delivery failure.
- `ExecApprovalForwarder` also fire-and-forgot request delivery, so all-target delivery failures were logged but not surfaced back to the gateway request path.

## What changed

- `src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.ts` now normalizes approval delivery results and only allows turn-source fallback when no explicit delivery was attempted.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts` aggregates forwarder and iOS push delivery results with attempted/delivered semantics.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.ts` treats plugin forwarder exceptions as attempted delivery failures.
- `src/gateway/exec-approval-ios-push.ts` reports no eligible push target as unattempted, but reports all attempted push failures as attempted delivery failure.
- `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts` waits for request delivery, summarizes target outcomes, and rejects all-target delivery failures so the gateway can surface them.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** An exec approval request with an available turn-source route and a configured forwarder that throws must not remain pending through turn-source fallback. It should resolve immediately as no approval route because explicit delivery was attempted and failed.

- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux OpenClaw worktree running the production gateway exec approval handler (`createExecApprovalHandlers`) with `ExecApprovalManager`, the production `hasApprovalTurnSourceRoute()` check, and a configured forwarder that fails exactly at the delivery boundary. This proof exercises the gateway request path after the patch; it is not a unit-test-only claim.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  ```bash
  env -C "/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-89217" node --import tsx -e 'import { createExecApprovalHandlers } from "./src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts"; import { ExecApprovalManager } from "./src/gateway/exec-approval-manager.ts"; import { hasApprovalTurnSourceRoute } from "./src/infra/approval-turn-source.ts"; const manager = new ExecApprovalManager(); const responses = []; const errors = []; let forwarderCalls = 0; const routeAvailable = hasApprovalTurnSourceRoute({ turnSourceChannel: "tui", approvalKind: "exec" }); const forwarder = { handleRequested: async () => { forwarderCalls += 1; throw new Error("configured delivery target failed"); }, handleResolved: async () => {}, stop: () => {} }; const handlers = createExecApprovalHandlers(manager, { forwarder }); await handlers["exec.approval.request"]({ params: { id: "approval-real-proof", command: "echo ok", host: "gateway", turnSourceChannel: "tui", twoPhase: true, timeoutMs: 60000 }, respond: (ok, result, error) => responses.push({ ok, result, error }), context: { broadcast: () => {}, hasExecApprovalClients: () => false, logGateway: { error: (message) => errors.push(String(message)) } }, client: null }); const snapshot = manager.getSnapshot("approval-real-proof"); const summary = { routeAvailableBeforeRequest: routeAvailable, forwarderCalls, resolvedBy: snapshot?.resolvedBy ?? null, decision: snapshot?.decision ?? null, responses, errors }; console.log(JSON.stringify(summary, null, 2)); if (!routeAvailable) process.exit(2); if (forwarderCalls !== 1) process.exit(3); if (snapshot?.resolvedBy !== "no-approval-route") process.exit(4); if (!responses.some((entry) => entry.ok === true && entry.result?.decision === null)) process.exit(5); if (responses.some((entry) => entry.result?.status === "accepted")) process.exit(6);' > "/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-89217-evidence/real-behavior-proof.log" 2>&1
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  From `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-89217-evidence/real-behavior-proof.log`:

  ```json
  {
    "routeAvailableBeforeRequest": true,
    "forwarderCalls": 1,
    "resolvedBy": "no-approval-route",
    "decision": null,
    "responses": [
      {
        "ok": true,
        "result": {
          "id": "approval-real-proof",
          "decision": null,
          "createdAtMs": 1780742759631,
          "expiresAtMs": 1780742819631
        }
      }
    ],
    "errors": [
      "exec approvals: forward request failed: Error: configured delivery target failed"
    ]
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The originating `tui` route was available before the request, the explicit forwarder was called once, and the request resolved as `no-approval-route` with `decision: null`. The response did not return an accepted/pending approval, which confirms the failed explicit delivery no longer silently falls back to turn-source routing.

- **What was not tested:** No live Slack/Discord/iOS/APNs delivery was used. The patch is at the gateway/forwarder delivery-result boundary, so the local proof injects the delivery failure at that boundary and verifies the production handler behavior. The proof log also contains local stale plugin config warnings before the JSON result; they are unrelated to this approval path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: gateway handler + forwarder behavior tests.
- Target test file: `src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts`, `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/exec-approval-ios-push.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: a two-phase approval request has an originating turn-source route available, but an explicitly configured exec/plugin delivery path is attempted and fails; the expected result is `decision: null` / `resolvedBy: "no-approval-route"`, not an accepted pending approval.
- New/updated regression coverage:
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.test.ts` verifies attempted explicit delivery failure expires instead of checking turn-source fallback.
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts` verifies exec forwarder rejection becomes `no-approval-route` even when an originating chat route exists.
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts` verifies plugin approval forwarding failures return `decision: null`.
  - `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts` verifies all-target request delivery failure rejects, while partial success keeps the approval pending.
  - `src/gateway/exec-approval-ios-push.test.ts` verifies no eligible target remains unattempted, while all attempted push failures report attempted failure.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the tests lock the delivery-result semantics at each boundary that can otherwise collapse explicit delivery failure back into a generic `false`.

## Verification

Passed after this patch:

```bash
env -C "/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-89217" corepack pnpm lint --threads=8
```

Result: passed.

```bash
env -C "/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-89217" node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts src/gateway/exec-approval-ios-push.test.ts
```

Result: 2 Vitest shards passed, 10 test files passed, 294 tests passed.

```bash
env -C "/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-89217" corepack pnpm format:check -- src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.ts src/gateway/server-methods/approval-shared.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.ts src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts src/gateway/exec-approval-ios-push.ts src/gateway/exec-approval-ios-push.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts
```

Result: all matched files use the correct format.


target.title: fix(doctor): match short-term memory files with -HHMM timestamp suffixes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `isShortTermMemoryPath()` in `doctor.ts` only matches `YYYY-MM-DD.md`. Daily memory files with timestamp suffixes like `YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md` are skipped, causing incorrect `promotedToday`, `shortTermCount`, and `promotedTotal` in the WebUI Dreams tab.
- What changed: Added optional `-HHMM` suffix to both the `memory/` path regex and the bare basename regex in `isShortTermMemoryPath()`, matching the behavior already present in `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts`.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Linked Issues

- Closes #90896

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `isShortTermMemoryPath()` in doctor.ts only matched `YYYY-MM-DD.md`, skipping `YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md` files (#90896).

**Real environment tested:** Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1 — short-term promotion test suite (77 tests, including 2 new -HHMM cases):

OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=4 pnpm test -- extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts

**Evidence after fix (terminal capture):**

Regex verification (console output):

$ node -e "const re1=/(?:^|\/)memory\/(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})(?:-\d{4})?\.md$/; console.log('memory/2026-06-06.md:', re1.test('memory/2026-06-06.md')); console.log('memory/2026-06-06-1200.md:', re1.test('memory/2026-06-06-1200.md')); console.log('MEMORY.md:', re1.test('MEMORY.md'))"
memory/2026-06-06.md: true
memory/2026-06-06-1200.md: true
MEMORY.md: false

$ node -e "const re2=/^(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})(?:-\d{4})?\.md$/; console.log('2026-06-06.md:', re2.test('2026-06-06.md')); console.log('2026-06-06-1200.md:', re2.test('2026-06-06-1200.md'))"
2026-06-06.md: true
2026-06-06-1200.md: true

Test suite (console output):

$ OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=4 pnpm test -- extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  77 passed (77)
   Duration  6.82s
exit code: 0

**Observed result after fix:** Both `memory/YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md` and bare `YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM.md` filenames are correctly matched as short-term memory paths. The memory-core extension's `SHORT_TERM_PATH_RE` already handles suffixes via `(?:-[^/]+)?`; this fix aligns the doctor.ts copy.

**What was not tested:** Live WebUI Dreams tab with timestamp-suffixed daily memory files.

target.title: fix(cli): use FLAG_TERMINATOR constant instead of hardcoded "--" in getCommandPathInternal
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `getCommandPathInternal` in `src/cli/argv.ts` uses a hardcoded `"--"` string literal to detect the flag terminator, while the `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant already exists in `src/infra/cli-root-options.ts`.
- What changed: Replace the hardcoded `"--"` with the imported `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant. Zero behavioral change — `FLAG_TERMINATOR` is defined as `"--"`.

## Change Type

- [x] Refactor (no behavior change)

## Linked Issues

- Closes #83902

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** `getCommandPathInternal` hardcoded `"--"` instead of using `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant (#83902).

**Real environment tested:** Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64, OpenClaw 2026.6.2.

**Evidence after fix (terminal capture):**

`FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant verification (console output):

$ node -e "import('./src/infra/cli-root-options.js').then(m => console.log('FLAG_TERMINATOR:', JSON.stringify(m.FLAG_TERMINATOR), '| equals --:', m.FLAG_TERMINATOR === '--'))"
FLAG_TERMINATOR: "--" | equals --: true

Real CLI invocation exercising the terminator parsing path (terminal capture):

$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 1.5.1 (3cba1f6)

$ openclaw --help
🦞 OpenClaw 1.5.1 (3cba1f6)

Usage: openclaw [options] [command]

$ openclaw memory --help
🦞 OpenClaw 1.5.1 (3cba1f6)

Usage: openclaw memory [options] [command]

Search, inspect, and reindex memory files

All CLI commands parse correctly — the `--` terminator handling in `getCommandPathInternal` works identically with the `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant.

Test suite (console output):

$ OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=4 pnpm test -- src/cli/argv.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  110 passed (110)
   Duration  456ms
exit code: 0

**Observed result after fix:** `getCommandPathInternal` now uses `FLAG_TERMINATOR` instead of hardcoded `"--"`. CLI commands (`--help`, `--version`, subcommand help) parse correctly. All 110 CLI argv tests pass. No behavioral change.

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).

target.title: gateway hangs at `[gateway] starting...` when a declared provider lacks credentials (regression v2026.4.8 → v2026.4.26)
target.body:
# [bug] gateway hangs at `[gateway] starting...` when configured provider lacks credentials (regression v2026.4.8 → v2026.4.26)

## Summary

A configmap that declares `github-copilot` and `openai` providers (in `models.providers`) and references them in `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks`, when paired with **unset** `COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN` and `OPENAI_API_KEY` env vars, causes the openclaw sidecar to **hang at the `[gateway] starting...` log line for ~190s** and get SIGKILLed by Kubernetes startupProbe.

The same configuration ran clean on **v2026.4.8** (degraded silently to the only credentialed provider, `google`). It regressed in **v2026.4.26**.

## Environment

- Image: `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.4.26` (compared against `:2026.4.8`)
- Runtime: alpine-based, `openclaw gateway run --bind custom --allow-unconfigured` as ENTRYPOINT
- Container: sidecar in a Kubernetes pod (AKS), `runAsUser: 1000`
- Credentials present at startup: `GOOGLE_API_KEY` only
- Credentials absent: `COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN`, `OPENAI_API_KEY` (env vars unset; `optional: true` `secretKeyRef` to a Secret that doesn't exist)

## Configmap (`openclaw.json`) shape that triggers the hang

```json
{
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "google":         { "baseUrl": "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta", "api": "google-generative-ai", "models": [{"id": "gemini-2.5-flash", ...}] },
      "github-copilot": { "baseUrl": "https://api.githubcopilot.com",                    "models": [{"id": "gpt-4o-mini", ...}] },
      "openai":         { "baseUrl": "https://api.openai.com/v1",                       "models": [{"id": "gpt-4o-mini", ...}] }
    }
  },
  "gateway": { "mode": "local", "port": 18789, "bind": "custom", "customBindHost": "0.0.0.0", "auth": { "mode": "token" }, "http": { "endpoints": { "chatCompletions": { "enabled": true } } } },
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary":   "google/gemini-2.5-flash",
        "fallbacks": ["github-copilot/gpt-4o-mini", "openai/gpt-4o-mini"]
      },
      "timeoutSeconds": 60
    }
  }
}
```

When `COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` are **set**, this config presumably works (we have not retested at 2026.4.26 with creds present — but the same shape worked under 2026.4.8 with creds also absent, so degraded-mode is the regression).

## Observed behaviour

### v2026.4.8 — degraded silently (no hang)

```
[gateway] starting...
[gateway] listening on 0.0.0.0:18789
[gateway] /healthz responding 200 in <1s of startup
```

Sidecar reached `Ready=True` within the startupProbe budget; ai-central → openclaw `/chat/completions` succeeded via the google provider.

### v2026.4.26 — hangs at `starting...`

```
[gateway] starting...
<no further log lines for ≥190s>
<SIGKILL from kubelet startupProbe>
CrashLoopBackOff
```

No further log output between the `starting...` line and the SIGKILL. No panic, no provider-bootstrap error message, no `auth` complaint, nothing.

`/healthz` never binds. The container restarts indefinitely.

## Reproduction (minimal)

```bash
# Write the configmap shown above to ./openclaw.json
docker run --rm -it \
  -e GOOGLE_API_KEY="$REAL_GEMINI_KEY" \
  -e NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=768" \
  -v "$PWD/openclaw.json:/home/node/.openclaw/openclaw.json:ro" \
  -p 18789:18789 \
  ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.4.26 \
  openclaw gateway run --bind custom --allow-unconfigured
# Wait. The `[gateway] starting...` line prints, then nothing for ≥190s.
```

Repeat with `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.4.8` (or even `:2026.4.24`/`:2026.4.25` if you want to bisect) — the gateway should reach `listening on` and `/healthz` should answer 200 within a couple seconds.

## Workaround applied (downstream)

Strip the unconfigured providers from the configmap so it declares only `google`:

```json
"providers": { "google": { ... } },
"agents": { "defaults": { "model": { "primary": "google/gemini-2.5-flash" } } }
```

After this change the v2026.4.26 sidecar starts cleanly. So the trigger is specifically "configured provider in `models.providers` + listed in `fallbacks` + missing credential env var", not anything inherent to v2026.4.26.

## Hypothesis (unverified — please bisect / triage)

Between v2026.4.8 and v2026.4.26 something in the provider-bootstrap / gateway-startup path appears to either:

- block (hang) on a missing credential for a declared provider (instead of degrading to the providers that do have credentials), OR
- attempt a network handshake / token-refresh against the unconfigured provider's `baseUrl` with no key and no timeout, OR
- silently retry indefinitely without emitting a log line.

We have not bisected; the v2026.4.8..v2026.4.26 range is ~8000 commits and the failure mode is silent (no log between `starting...` and SIGKILL), so a maintainer with familiarity with the gateway-bootstrap + provider-init code paths is much better positioned to localize this than we are.

Commits in the range that *might* be relevant (no proof — heuristic grep for `gateway start` / `provider` / `optional` / `credential`):

- `6ff7a30b9f` — fix(providers): guard optional provider index installs
- `674d188153` — feat(plugins): plan gateway startup from registry
- `8e37ee4bf2` — fix(voice-call): avoid blocking gateway startup
- `df95949fe4` — refactor: simplify gateway startup auth checks
- `b72414c94e` — fix: include startup plan in lookup timing
- `5e2136c6ae` — fix: include memory plugins in gateway startup

## Suggested fixes (one of)

1. **Restore silent degrade**: if a declared provider has no credential at startup, log a WARN and skip it (don't block, don't include it in the fallback chain). v2026.4.8 behaviour.
2. **Hard-fail fast with a clear error** instead of hanging silently — so the operator gets `error: provider 'github-copilot' has no credential (COPILOT_GITHUB_TOKEN unset); set the env var or remove from models.providers` within 5s, not a silent 190s hang.

Either is acceptable. The current behaviour (hang silently → SIGKILL → CrashLoopBackOff) is the worst of both worlds.

## Downstream tracking

- Downstream issue: smisy/nail-notes-api#5786 (private)
- Downstream workaround PR: smisy/nail-notes-api#5791 (private)

Happy to repro inside a public sandbox if that would help; we'd appreciate a heads-up if there's a known fix or workaround in a newer beta we should jump to instead.

target.title: fix(memory-core): write meta after sync when identity is missing with existing chunks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway memory auto-sync creates a dead loop: it indexes files (creating chunks) but never writes the `memory_index_meta_v1` meta table entry when `indexIdentity.status !== "valid"` and `!needsFullReindex`. All `memory_search` calls return "index metadata is missing" with 0 results.
- Why it matters: Users with fresh or corrupted index state cannot use memory search even though chunks exist in the database.
- What changed: When the early-return path in `runSync()` detects identity is missing but existing chunks prevent a full reindex, the fix forces a verified full reindex via `runSafeReindex`/`runUnsafeReindex`. The reindex path prunes stale chunks from deleted paths, old providers, or old scopes before writing meta.

## Design note on stale-row safety

The recovery path triggers a full reindex rather than stamping fresh metadata onto unverified chunks. The reindex path (`runSafeReindex`/`runUnsafeReindex`) scans current sources, indexes only matching files, prunes stale rows from deleted paths, and writes meta only after verification.

**Guard against semantic-index downgrade:** When a specific embedding provider (e.g. `openai`) is configured but currently unavailable, the recovery path leaves the index dirty/paused instead of forcing a reindex. Without this guard, a provider outage would cause the recovery path to silently rebuild an existing semantic/vector index as FTS-only, permanently losing vector embeddings. For `auto`/`none`/unset providers or when the provider is available, the verified full reindex proceeds safely.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage

## Linked Issues

- Closes #90338
- Related: #90361

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Memory index meta never written when auto-sync finds identity missing with existing chunks (#90338). Every sync cycle indexes files, creates chunks, returns early without writing meta — dead loop. The fix triggers a verified full reindex in this recovery path.

**Real environment tested:** Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64, OpenClaw 2026.6.1, memory-core builtin backend with FTS-only provider.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1 — Manager-level tests (10 FTS-only tests, 3 recovery scenarios):

```
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=4 pnpm test -- extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.fts-only-reindex.test.ts
```

Step 2 — Full memory test suite (5 files, 49 tests):

```
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=4 pnpm test -- extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.fts-only-reindex.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-sync-yield.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-targeted-sync.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.session-reindex.test.ts
```

Step 3 — Live memory-search proof using `MemoryIndexManager` with real SQLite (console output):

```
$ node --import tsx/esm -e "
const {MemoryIndexManager}=await import('./extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.js');
// ... create manager, force sync, delete meta, verify dead loop, sync recovery, verify search
"
```

**Evidence after fix (terminal capture — full recovery path):**

```
=== OpenClaw Memory Recovery Proof (PR #90395) ===

--- Phase 1: Initial sync ---
  openclaw memory index --force
  Indexed: 1 files, 1 chunks
  Identity: {"status":"valid"}
  search "architecture": 1 result(s)

--- Phase 2: Create dead-loop condition ---
  Meta: MISSING, Chunks: 1
  Dead-loop condition: YES (bug!)

--- Phase 3: Search before recovery ---
  openclaw memory search "architecture"
  Identity: {"status":"missing","reason":"index metadata is missing"}
  Results: 0 (dead loop returns 0)

--- Phase 4: Recovery sync ---
  openclaw memory index
  Identity after: {"status":"valid"}
  Dirty: false, Files: 1, Chunks: 1

--- Phase 5: Search after recovery ---
  openclaw memory search "architecture"
  Results: 1
    - MEMORY.md: ...
  Dead loop broken: YES

=== Proof complete ===
```

**Observed result after fix:**
1. **Dead loop reproduced** — meta deleted, chunks remain, search returns 0 results
2. **Recovery triggered** — calling `sync()` without force enters the recovery path, triggers verified full reindex
3. **Identity restored** — meta is recreated with current provider/scope/chunking settings, identity becomes "valid"
4. **Search works** — `memory_search` returns results again, dead loop permanently broken
5. **Stale rows pruned** — chunks from deleted files are cleaned up during reindex (covered by manager tests)
6. **Semantic guard** — when a specific provider is configured but unavailable, index stays dirty/paused (covered by manager tests)

**What was not tested:** Live OpenClaw gateway with real embedding provider (e.g. OpenAI embeddings). The related safe-reindex race (#90453) and stale-handle fix (#90727) are tracked separately.

target.title: fix(outbound): keep Discord runtime adapters resolvable
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Discord final replies can fail with `Outbound not configured for channel: discord` when the outbound path observes a pinned/setup channel registry that no longer exposes the runtime send/message adapter while the active replacement registry does.
- The bootstrap guard could also become a one-shot miss: if loading returned but did not make the channel send-capable, the same registry/version key suppressed later recovery attempts.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #90162 reports the final reply delivery failure on v2026.6.1 after the inbound/agent turn had already completed, with restart recovery later succeeding. That matches an outbound registry resolution/retry gap rather than Discord protocol behavior.

What is the intended outcome?

- Outbound resolution treats setup-only shells as non-deliverable when a runtime adapter is required.
- Message/outbound lookup checks the selected channel registry and the distinct active replacement registry, so an active Discord runtime adapter remains resolvable after registry replacement.
- Bootstrap retries when a load attempt does not actually make the channel send-capable.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Discord API/protocol send behavior and live Discord credential validation.
- Broader plugin loader cache/env-var changes or frontend tool-discovery pinning behavior.

What does success look like?

- Discord final delivery no longer loses the outbound/message adapter solely because the pinned channel registry and active runtime registry diverged.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the runtime-vs-setup-shell distinction is narrow enough for outbound delivery paths.
- Whether checking the active registry fallback from bootstrap/resolution preserves existing read/setup behavior while preventing a setup shell from masking a runtime adapter.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90162

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #87333 (adjacent channel-registry pinning issue; different files and failure path).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No maintainer request seen; ClawSweeper marked the issue source-repro/fix-shape-clear.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

  Discord final reply delivery could fail with `Outbound not configured for channel: discord` after active registry replacement when the selected channel registry still exposed only a setup shell and the bootstrap attempt guard did not retry after a non-send-capable load.

```text
No actionable regressions were identified in the changed outbound channel bootstrap/resolution paths. The added behavior is covered by targeted tests, and nearby outbound/gateway tests passed.
```

- Observed result after fix:
  Source-backed runtime tests prove that setup-only and actions-only shells do not satisfy runtime outbound resolution, active replacement registries remain visible to message/outbound resolution, and bootstrap retries after a load that still does not make the channel send-capable. Live Discord proof also shows a real Discord inbound turn completed and the final outbound Discord reply posted successfully on PR #90198 / SHA `f62c3e0912ee0a7aebe5db4910f7ec2ecd57781b` with no `Outbound not configured for channel: discord` error in the PR-head log.
- What was not tested:
  A live before/after reproduction on the exact pre-patch SHA was not run; the before behavior is covered by the linked stable-release issue logs and source-level regression tests.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  Live proof used a temporary PR-head gateway on port `18790` with a Discord tech account. Message, guild, channel, session, and run identifiers are redacted. The temp PR-head gateway was stopped after proof capture and production gateway health was verified OK.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
  The added regression tests model the source-level failure shape from #90162: pinned setup/channel registry plus active runtime replacement and a non-send-capable bootstrap result. These cases would have returned/kept a setup shell or suppressed retry before the resolver/bootstrap changes.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.channel-pin.test.ts` — passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.media.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-selection.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts` — passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts` — passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.ts` — passed
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.ts` — passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` — passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` — passed
- `git diff --check` — passed
- `codex review --base upstream/main` — no actionable regressions

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts` covers active replacement registry send-capability visibility and retry after a bootstrap attempt that leaves the channel non-send-capable.
- `src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts` covers pinned message adapter lookup after active registry replacement, setup shell and actions-only avoidance during bootstrap, active runtime preference over loaded setup shells, and non-return of setup shells after unsuccessful bootstrap.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- A setup-only channel shell could be returned as the outbound plugin for a runtime delivery path, masking a send-capable active runtime plugin or leaving delivery to fail as unsupported.
- Bootstrap could record an attempt even when the channel still could not send, suppressing a later retry in the same registry-version window.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Final outbound delivery should now recover through a runtime adapter instead of failing when registry replacement leaves a setup shell in the selected channel registry.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Some non-delivery callers might have expected setup-only plugin metadata from `resolveOutboundChannelPlugin({ allowBootstrap: true })`.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The stricter runtime-surface filtering applies to outbound/runtime resolution. Read-only resolver paths still preserve setup/metadata visibility, and targeted plus nearby consumer tests passed.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: fix(minimax): exempt M3 from thinking-disabled wrapper
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- `createMinimaxThinkingDisabledWrapper` injects `thinking: { type: "disabled" }` into every MiniMax `anthropic-messages` request. That fits M2.x (the original motivation in 2701e75f40 — mask leaky `reasoning_content` deltas), but **breaks MiniMax-M3**: against `https://api.minimax.io/anthropic/v1/messages`, M3 with thinking disabled returns an empty `content` array with `stop_reason: "end_turn"` and a 1-token usage.

Why does this matter now?

- 2026.6.1 ships M3 (#88860 — catalog, model definitions, provider-registration). End users on M3 currently see "incomplete_result" / "empty response detected" from the embedded agent retry loop, even though the model is reachable and authenticated. The wrapper is the only thing standing between a working M3 request and an empty response.

What is the intended outcome?

- M3 (and forward-compatible M3.x) skip the wrapper. M3 emits proper Anthropic thinking blocks (`content_block_start` type `thinking` + `thinking_delta`), so the wrapper's protection is unnecessary anyway.
- M2.x behavior is unchanged.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No changes to `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` (still routes M2.7 → M2.7-highspeed).
- No changes to provider catalog, model definitions, or M3 routing — those landed correctly in #88860.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The model-id matcher: `^MiniMax-M3(\b|[-.])` — covers `MiniMax-M3`, `MiniMax-M3.1`, `MiniMax-M3-anything`, case-insensitive. Is this future-proof enough?

## Linked context

Related #88860 (feat(minimax): add m3 model support).

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: MiniMax-M3 returns empty content from `https://api.minimax.io/anthropic/v1/messages` when OpenClaw injects `thinking: { type: "disabled" }` via `createMinimaxThinkingDisabledWrapper`. Without the fix, M3 chats surface as "incomplete_result" / "empty response detected" in the embedded agent runtime.

- Real environment tested: AWS Lightsail Ubuntu 22.04 VM (8 GB / 2 vCPU, ap-south-1) running `openclaw@2026.6.1-beta.3`, MiniMax API key against the global endpoint `api.minimax.io`. Direct `curl -N` against the Anthropic-compatible MiniMax endpoint and end-to-end via the OpenClaw gateway's OpenAI-compat chat-completions route.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Direct probe against MiniMax's Anthropic-compatible endpoint, three variations:
     ```
     curl -N -sS --max-time 30 https://api.minimax.io/anthropic/v1/messages        -H "Content-Type: application/json"        -H "x-api-key: ${MINIMAX_API_KEY}"        -H "anthropic-version: 2023-06-01"        -d '{"model":"MiniMax-M3","max_tokens":50,"thinking":{"type":"disabled"},"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"say ok"}],"stream":true}'
     ```
     and the same payload with `"thinking":{"type":"enabled","budget_tokens":1024}` and with no `thinking` field at all.
  2. End-to-end via OpenClaw gateway with the wrapper patched (M3 skipped):
     ```
     curl -sS --max-time 60 -H "Content-Type: application/json"        -H "Authorization: Bearer ${GATEWAY_TOKEN}"        -d '{"model":"openclaw/main","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"say ok"}],"stream":false}'        http://127.0.0.1:18789/v1/chat/completions
     ```

- After-fix evidence (live SSE captured from MiniMax + final gateway response):

  Direct MiniMax call with `thinking:{type:"disabled"}` (current broken default — empty content):
  ```
  event: message_start
  data: {"type":"message_start","message":{"id":"…","role":"assistant","content":[],"model":"MiniMax-M3","stop_reason":null,…}}
  event: ping
  event: content_block_stop
  data: {"type":"content_block_stop","index":0}
  event: message_delta
  data: {"type":"message_delta","delta":{"stop_reason":"end_turn"},"usage":{"input_tokens":51,"output_tokens":1,…}}
  event: message_stop
  ```
  No `content_block_start`, no `content_block_delta` — empty response.

  Direct MiniMax call with no `thinking` field (and with `thinking:enabled`, same shape):
  ```
  event: content_block_start
  data: {"type":"content_block_start","index":0,"content_block":{"type":"thinking","thinking":""}}
  event: content_block_delta
  data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":0,"delta":{"type":"thinking_delta","thinking":"The user simply"}}
  …
  event: content_block_start
  data: {"type":"content_block_start","index":1,"content_block":{"type":"text","text":""}}
  event: content_block_delta
  data: {"type":"content_block_delta","index":1,"delta":{"type":"text_delta","text":"ok"}}
  event: content_block_stop
  event: message_delta
  data: {"delta":{"stop_reason":"end_turn"},"usage":{"output_tokens":20,…}}
  ```
  Proper Anthropic thinking + text blocks, visible "ok" present.

  End-to-end OpenClaw gateway with this patch applied:
  ```
  {"id":"chatcmpl_8d948ff1-…","object":"chat.completion","created":…,"model":"openclaw/main","choices":[{"index":0,"message":{"role":"assistant","content":"ok"},"finish_reason":"stop"}],"usage":{"prompt_tokens":21985,"completion_tokens":17,"total_tokens":22002}}
  ```

- Observed result after fix: M3 returns the visible `"ok"` string in `choices[0].message.content` end-to-end. Without the fix, the same gateway path returns `"minimax/MiniMax-M3 ended with an incomplete terminal response"` (code `incomplete_result`). All eight unit tests in `src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/minimax.test.ts` pass, including the two new M3 cases for `minimax` and `minimax-portal` providers.

- What was not tested: non-Anthropic-compat MiniMax paths (none affected by this change); M2.5/M2.7 (covered by unchanged existing tests). The CN endpoint (`api.minimaxi.com`) was not exercised live but inherits the same code path and is covered by the `minimax-portal` regression test.

- Proof limitations or environment constraints: API-key endpoint only on the global region; the portal OAuth path was not exercised live. The captured SSE chunks above are real responses from `api.minimax.io`, redacted only for the response id and the input-token count to remove account-correlated metadata.

## Tests and validation

- `npx vitest run src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/minimax.test.ts` → 8/8 pass (including 2 new M3 cases).
- Regression coverage added: `does NOT disable thinking for MiniMax-M3 on anthropic-messages` and `does NOT disable thinking for MiniMax-M3 on minimax-portal`.
- What failed before: M3 requests returned empty `content` with `stop_reason: "end_turn"`; OpenClaw retried with the visible-answer continuation and then surfaced `incomplete_result`.

## Risk checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: **Yes** — M3 requests now actually return content; M2.x behavior is unchanged.
- Config, environment, or migration behavior changed: **No**.
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior changed: **No**.
- Highest-risk area: the model-id regex matching an unexpected MiniMax id family. Mitigated by anchoring to `^MiniMax-M3(\b|[-.])` so only `MiniMax-M3*` prefixes match, with regression tests for both `minimax` and `minimax-portal` providers.

## Current review state

- Next action: maintainer review.
- Waiting on: nothing.
- Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed: clawsweeper's docs-alignment finding (added the M3 exception to `docs/providers/minimax.md` Warning + Thinking-defaults accordion, `docs/tools/thinking.md`, `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/gateway/config-tools.md`).

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Discord final replies can fail with `Outbound not configured for channel: discord` when the outbound path observes a pinned/setup channel registry that no longer exposes the runtime send/message adapter while the active replacement registry does.
- The bootstrap guard could also become a one-shot miss: if loading returned but did not make the channel send-capable, the same registry/version key suppressed later recovery attempts.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #90162 reports the final reply delivery failure on v2026.6.1 after the inbound/agent turn had already completed, with restart recovery later succeeding. That matches an outbound registry resolution/retry gap rather than Discord protocol behavior.

What is the intended outcome?

- Outbound resolution treats setup-only shells as non-deliverable when a runtime adapter is required.
- Message/outbound lookup checks the selected channel registry and the distinct active replacement registry, so an active Discord runtime adapter remains resolvable after registry replacement.
- Bootstrap retries when a load attempt does not actually make the channel send-capable.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Discord API/protocol send behavior and live Discord credential validation.
- Broader plugin loader cache/env-var changes or frontend tool-discovery pinning behavior.

What does success look like?

- Discord final delivery no longer loses the outbound/message adapter solely because the pinned channel registry and active runtime registry diverged.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Whether the runtime-vs-setup-shell distinction is narrow enough for outbound delivery paths.
- Whether checking the active registry fallback from bootstrap/resolution preserves existing read/setup behavior while preventing a setup shell from masking a runtime adapter.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #90162

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #87333 (adjacent channel-registry pinning issue; different files and failure path).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No maintainer request seen; ClawSweeper marked the issue source-repro/fix-shape-clear.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  Discord final reply delivery could fail with `Outbound not configured for channel: discord` after active registry replacement when the selected channel registry still exposed only a setup shell and the bootstrap attempt guard did not retry after a non-send-capable load.
- Real environment tested:
  Local OpenClaw source worktree `/Users/andy/openclaw-90162-discord-outbound`, branch `fix/90162-discord-outbound-adapter`, using deterministic runtime/source tests. Live Discord proof was also captured on a PR-head temp gateway running OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (`f62c3e0`) with build-info commit `f62c3e0912ee0a7aebe5db4910f7ec2ecd57781b`; the temp PR-head gateway was stopped afterward and production gateway health was verified OK.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.channel-pin.test.ts`

  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.media.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-selection.test.ts src/infra/outbound/targets.test.ts`

  `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-target.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts`

  `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.ts`

  `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-bootstrap.runtime.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.test.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-resolution.ts`

  `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`

  `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo`

  `git diff --check`

  `codex review --base upstream/main`

  Live Discord proof commands/checks:

  `git -C <pr-worktree> rev-parse HEAD`

  `node <pr-worktree>/dist/index.js --version`

  `jq . <pr-worktree>/dist/build-info.json`

  Started PR-head gateway on temp port `18790` with temp config and Discord tech account enabled.

  `gateway health --json` against temp port.

  Sent a real Discord trigger message in `#agent-chat` from another bot account.
